title: The Urgent Invoice
briefing: Detective, a retired accountant received this email this morning and nearly clicked the link before something felt off. The sender claims to be Amazon — but your trained eye knows that names can be faked. Examine every detail before it's too late.

evidence.html:
From: security-alert@amaz0n-support.com
To: customer@email.com
Subject: ⚠️ URGENT: Your Amazon Account Has Been Compromised — Immediate Action Required
Date: April 15, 2026 — 7:43 AM
amazon

Dear Valued Customer,

We have detected unauthorized access to your Amazon account from an unrecognized device. To protect your account and prevent permanent suspension, you must verify your identity within 24 hours.

If you do not verify immediately, your account will be permanently closed and all pending orders will be cancelled.

Verify Your Account Now

You will be asked to confirm your: password, billing address, and credit card number to complete verification.

Amazon Customer Service
© 2026 amaz0n-support.com. All rights reserved.

hotspots.label: Fake sender domain — amaz0n-support.com
hotspots.explanation: The real Amazon always sends email from @amazon.com. Notice the '0' (zero) instead of 'o' in 'amaz0n'. This is a lookalike domain — a classic phishing trick to fool a quick glance.

hotspots.label: Generic greeting — 'Dear Valued Customer'
hotspots.explanation: Amazon knows your name — they use it in every real email. 'Dear Valued Customer' means the sender doesn't actually know who you are. Real companies personalize their emails.

hotspots.label: Threatening language — 'account will be permanently closed'
hotspots.explanation: Threats of immediate, permanent consequences are designed to panic you into acting without thinking. Legitimate companies give you time and multiple ways to resolve issues.

hotspots.label: Suspicious link URL — amazon-account-verify.suspicious-site.net
hotspots.explanation: Hover over the button before clicking — the real destination is not amazon.com. Scammers make buttons look official while hiding dangerous URLs underneath.

hotspots.label: Requests password, credit card, and SSN
hotspots.explanation: Amazon never asks for your password by email. No legitimate company asks for your full credit card number in an email. This is designed to harvest your financial information.

deduction_options.text: Phishing email — the fake domain, generic greeting, threats, and suspicious link all confirm this is a scam designed to steal account credentials
Legitimate Amazon security alert — the account really has been compromised and needs immediate action
Spam marketing email — harmless but annoying promotional content

learning_summary: Check the sender's actual email address — not just the display name. Never click links in urgent emails. Go directly to amazon.com by typing it yourself, then check your account. Real companies never ask for passwords by email.
